column8: Assessment tools used to capture details about the caregiver network

column2: Do Caregiving Factors Affect Hospitalization Risk Among Disabled Older Adults?
column5: Total participants: 2589, Subgroups: Older adults with dementia: 925, Older adults without dementia: 1664
column6: Caregivers are providing help to older adults with self-care or mobility disabilities, with 31% of these older adults having dementia.
column7: Primary caregivers are defined as family or unpaid nonfamily caregivers who help the participant the most. In the NHATS-linked NSOC, the primary caregiver is the one providing the most hours of help weekly among the caregivers interviewed.
column8: Caregiver characteristics and caregiving circumstances were self-reported in ICS and NSOC surveys., Details assessed included caregiver age, sex, relationship to the older adult (spouse or nonspouse), duration of caregiving, living arrangement, employment status, hours of care provided weekly, type of help provided (self-care, mobility, healthcare tasks), and self-rated health., Caregiving challenges such as financial strain, emotional strain, and physical strain were also evaluated., Caregiving support included use of support groups and respite care.
column9: Caregiver-reported physical strain, emotional strain, and financial strain., Duration of caregiving., Weekly hours of care provided., Type of help provided (self-care, mobility, healthcare tasks).

column2: Impact of Noncaregiving-Related Stressors on Informal Caregiver Outcomes
column5: Total dyads: 153, Intervention arm dyads: 84, Caregivers completing Most Stressful Event (MSE) form: 31
column6: Caregivers of older adults with Alzheimer's disease and dementia.
column7: Caregivers were primarily spouses (80%) and adult children, with inclusion criteria focusing on family caregivers participating in a psychosocial educational support group as part of a collaborative dementia care trial. Caregivers were asked to report their most stressful events during monthly support group meetings.
column8: The study did not explicitly assess the caregiver network but included caregiver demographics (age, gender, relationship to care recipient, education level) and stressors reported during support group meetings. Stressors were categorized into direct caregiving stressors, indirect caregiving stressors, noncaregiving stressors, and no stressors.
column9: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9): A 9-item self-report measure of caregiver depression severity., Revised Memory and Behavioral Problems Checklist (R-MBPC): A 24-item caregiver report measure assessing frequency of cognitive, functional, behavioral, and psychological symptoms of the care recipient, as well as caregiver reactions to these symptoms., Most Stressful Events (MSE): Open-ended responses to the question 'What are the three most stressful things in your life right now?'

column2: Caregiving Situation as a Predictor of Subjective Caregiver Burden: Informal Caregivers of Older Adults during the COVID-19 Pandemic
column6: Caregivers are providing help to older adults aged 65+ during the COVID-19 pandemic, including those with dementia, chronic diseases, disabilities, and other health conditions.
column7: Informal caregivers aged 40+ providing at least four hours per week of unpaid assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs), instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs), and/or emotional support to family members aged 65+.
column8: The study assessed caregiving situations using variables such as provision of ADLs and IADLs, type of caregiver (primary, secondary, or shared responsibility), care duration, average hours of care per week, formal care use, and care recipient’s health problems. Data were collected via an online survey and adapted from the Eurofamcare study and COVID-19 study.
column9: Self-Rated Burden (SRB) scale, ranging from 0 (not at all straining) to 10 (much too straining)., Caregiving intensity (e.g., hours of care per week, duration of care)., Provision of ADLs and IADLs., Impact of care recipient’s health conditions (e.g., dementia, chronic disease, terminal illness) on caregiver burden.

column2: Effects of Depressive & Anxious Symptoms on Norepinephrine and Platelet P-Selectin Responses to Acute Psychological Stress among Elderly Caregivers
column6: Caregivers providing in-home care for spouses with Alzheimer's disease.
column7: Caregivers were defined as individuals providing in-home care for spouses diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease, with an average duration of caregiving of 9 years. Participants taking anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, and beta-blockers were excluded initially, though some began taking beta-blockers after enrollment.
column8: The study did not explicitly assess the caregiver network or team. It focused on individual caregivers and their physiological and psychological responses to stress.
column9: Brief Symptom Inventory (BSI) depressive and anxious symptom subscales., Role Overload scale., Norepinephrine levels (reactivity and recovery) measured via blood samples., Platelet P-selectin expression (reactivity and recovery) measured via flow cytometry.
